trajectory_summary: The call opened on the strongest possible footing — inbound, motivated, financially activated, ideologically primed — and Parker converted it to a booked appointment. The trajectory is net positive but leaked energy in two places: a ~9-minute bill hunt that Parker managed passively rather than actively, and a missed title/co-owners check that leaves the appointment structurally exposed. The roofing cross-sell was identified but not completed. None of these gaps killed the call; they represent recoverable coaching targets on an otherwise clean booking.
outcome_dispute: The call ends with a booked appointment for Wednesday at 5:00 PM. The v1 tag is 'unknown' — this should be reclassified as 'appointment_booked.' The appointment is real but carries a structural vulnerability: no title/co-owners check was completed, meaning a spouse or co-owner on the title could surface at the closer stage and derail the sit.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Bill anchor flip — capture without emotional alignment
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Well, it's been skyrocketing, and I'm not like, what the heck is going on? Like, the past three months. But it's been it's been stable at 500, and it's never been 500.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Yasser handed Parker the single most emotionally activated moment in the call — a $500 bill that doubled from his $249 historical average, delivered with genuine frustration. Parker acknowledged it and moved to the credit qualifier. The flip (naming the utility as the adversary, tying the $500 to what solar replaces) was available with numbers already in the transcript and required no fabrication. This is the universal floor coaching gap the schema identifies — every rep captures the bill, no rep flips it. Parker had an unusually clean opportunity here because Yasser's own language ('skyrocketing,' 'never been 500') did half the work. Use this call as the teaching tape for the flip sequence.

key_moments.quote: And it's like what we was talking about in the seminar that I went recently. They were, like, the government's not gonna help you out or try to figure it out for you. You gotta figure that out for yourself.
key_moments.stewart_read: Yasser is not just bill-pained — he has been ideologically activated by a recent seminar and arrived on this call already framing solar as personal agency against a system that won't help him. Parker's response ('gotcha') acknowledged the statement and moved immediately to the credit qualifier. The flip was sitting right there: Yasser's own framing ('you gotta figure that out for yourself') is the exact emotional language the bill anchor should ride. Parker captured the bill pain earlier but never returned to this ideological frame to deepen the emotional alignment-against-utility.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. We could totally can we do roofing as well? So we could look at and we would have someone look at it for you before we, you know, get get going completely so then we could see what would be the best fit for you so we can kinda look at both for you.
key_moments.stewart_read: Parker identified the roofing signal and mentioned Ion's capability and bundling savings — this is a partially executed proactive move, not a reactive one. Parker had awareness of the cross-sell. What Parker did not do is explicitly co-route to the roofing team as the schema requires ('Want me to have our roofing team take a look?'). The distinction matters: this is a training gap on the routing step, not an awareness gap. Parker knows Ion does roofing; Parker does not yet have the habit of treating the roofing team co-route as a required action, not an optional mention.

key_moments.quote: I think I got two neighbors bad neighbors. They they have solar panels already.
key_moments.stewart_read: Neighbor adoption is a social proof signal that reinforces Yasser's motivation and implicitly validates the decision he is already leaning toward. Parker's response ('Okay. Looking...') was focused on the bill image and did not acknowledge the neighbor signal at all. This is a low-cost anchor point — a single sentence tying Yasser's neighbors' decision to his own would have deepened commitment before the close.

key_moments.quote: But, yeah, no rush. So no no worries there.
key_moments.stewart_read: At approximately the 3-minute mark of bill friction, Parker explicitly removed urgency with 'no rush, no worries.' This is the opposite of the schema's 2-minute pivot discipline. The correct move at this point was to offer the deferred-send path or pivot to verbal readback — not to signal that the hunt can continue indefinitely. Parker did eventually offer the deferred-send path at 15:21, but only after ~9 additional minutes of friction. Yasser is not elderly or low-tech — he navigated a desktop website and sent screenshots successfully. The issue is Parker's clock management, not the customer's capability.
